item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The images that were used in this article have been replaced with images that are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and are sourced as follows:

+ Show attribution
- Hide attribution

Title: 1929-new.jpg
Author(s): Het Nieuwe Instituut - Architecture Collection, Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck as an edited version of the image below

Source: Flickr
License: Public Domain
Title: Family dog and Gispen chair
Author: Het Nieuwe Instituut - Architecture Collection

Title: 1929-2-new.jpg
Author(s): N/A, Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck as an edited version of the image below

Source: pxhere
License: CC0
Title: N/A
Author: N/A

Title: 1929-3-new.jpg
Author(s): Joy ndr, Rocky Sun, Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck utilizing the images below as components

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Title: Ugly cat.jpg
Author: 2018

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: Lips - 364 day 23
Author: Rocky Sun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna have to agree with all the others saying that this page needs work. The sheer quantity of traits mentioned in passing but never coherently described reaches a point where, for me it was difficult to finish reading. I think that without the whole "turning people into animals" thing, this would be greatly improved, as it just doesn't seem to fit—unless the author can find some way to tie it into the rest of the article and explain it further. Is there any pattern to which people/creatures are affected how, for example? It's a -1 from me, pending any changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is steam rising
From the gravel on the road
There is black coal filling up our homes.
It seeps through the window glass
It slips through the floorboards
The mortar is turning black
The foundation starts to crack
Honey, pack your bags and go.

Steam Rising by Murder By Death is what I feel when I read this.
(I also like the Silent Hill amateur version.)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show block
- Hide block

The crosslink, suggested by the author of the page, takes into account the similar natures of SCP-165 and SCP-1929 in regards to dust and sand. As the anomalous effects are quite different, I have placed the proposed link in the description of 1929's discovery, as the confusion of Foundation personnel upon encountering a new anomaly seemed to be the most plausible method of placing the crosslink.

Editing this:

SCP-1929's effect was first noted by Foundation personnel after reports of it reached personnel embedded in the local weather stations. When personnel were sent to investigate, they discovered several persons who had been altered by SCP-1929. When asked to testify, residents gave contradictory reports ranging from seeing a person standing outside in the storm, to some other non-humanoid organism obscured by dust.

To this:

SCP-1929's effect was first noted by Foundation personnel after reports of it reached personnel embedded in the local weather stations. Concerns were initially raised over apparent similarity with infestations of SCP-165, although further examination of the anomaly would later show no connection. When personnel were sent to investigate, they discovered several persons who had been altered by SCP-1929. When asked to testify, residents gave contradictory reports ranging from seeing a person standing outside in the storm, to some other non-humanoid organism obscured by dust.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there's supposed to be an idea underneath it all that seems to make sense to people from a specific region of the US of A, but without their context it feels like a failed attempt at "thing wot makes anomalous items out of people and their things, so here's some random unrelated pictures". Not that "random unrelated pictures" are bad per se, there are SCPs that use them effectively (like that creepy Ukrainian apartment with the fetus-thing), it's just that here the hook that ties them together… Doesn't.

Also, on mobile the picture boxes are borked, with the first word of the paragraph following the box being to the left of the box instead of with the rest of the paragraph. If it matters, I'm using Chrome on an Android phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who grew up in an area where they teach about the Dust Bowl in elementary, middle, AND high school, I can say I vastly enjoyed this. Take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
altered to be similar to domesticated animals,

This feels like a really pointless addition, really, considering how you never touch upon it any further. That dog photo could just as well be part of the general WTFery.

Otherwise, this is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how you use the photographs, but the second one seems a bit off. I'm not entirely sure what the significance is to the SCP article as a whole.

Also, what did you mean by "had their bodies modified…"? Some revisions should be made for clarity.

I really don't think the interview added much to the article. In fact, I think it took away from the tone of the file. If you could add the same information without the interview, that'd be awesome.

It seems to ramble a bit in places. It wouldn't hurt to go back over the article and removed nonessential information. A general rule of writing is that only what's necessary is… necessary. ;)

Overall, this was highly entertaining! Upvoted! :) Although, I hope you take the time to improve it… I know it's old, but I should like to think you care to improve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others have said before me, the pictures are unneeded and muddy the article. The base of the SCP has a HUGE amount of potential, but it needs to either be trimmed down, or re-written with a more steady style and story. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These pictures are pretty unnecessary, as others have said. This article isn't focused on the animal behaviors, so the pictures make it kind of confusing.

Still, they're pretty damn scary…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually kind of love this because of the incoherence. It reminds me of the kind of nightmares where terrifyingly surreal things are happening for absolutely no reason and the sheer lack of logic is what's frightening. Something happened to all these towns so the whole area is literally a living nightmare, and the residents can't wake up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hook is workable. Narrow your focus, make it more coherent.

I recommend a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the living dust is silicon based life. I'm not sure what it's supposed to be doing. Heck, it might not be sure either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would help things make more sense, but it didn't come across very clearly. I agree with judgedeadd and others: this just feels too jumbled up, and the pictures seem too random. Definitely needs a trim and some restructuring, like you said.

No vote from me.

item.title: Re: Backing up...
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to agree with Esko, actually. I personally read hyphens with less of a pause than a dash, and such a change does make the interview log read differently.

Even though the single hyphens, as you say, have no meaning, I interpreted that they indicated that the speaker was interrupted relatively quickly. I'm not sure how else that would be portrayed, since a dash doesn't suit that sort of quick cut off.

item.title: Backing up...
item.content:encoded: We're drifting from the point. Never mind what's major or not; the issue at hand first was that a dash is represented properly by a double hyphen when em/en-dashes are inaccessible (or like in my case, when too lazy to look up alt codes). The single hyphens there don't have any meaning.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: It's a stylistic edit. It affects the way the reader reads the dialogue. That's major. I alerted him to it because I didn't know if he had given you permission or not. He hadn't. When in doubt, ask the author. When not in doubt, start doubting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a strange definition of "major".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was referring to the fact that you did a fairly major revision without my permission, and did it in a way I didn't like the look of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Roget 22 Mar 2013 ur doin it wrong

WHAT. A dash is two hyphens, not one. I had it done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the people confused about what's tying everything together: I think what the author was going for is:

The original dust storm was composed of animate dust, it is now turning the area into animate dust. The cat-lady, dog-man, flying scissors, etc. are all either reassimilations of former citizens of the town or them using their disconnected dust particles to control shit.

Really cool, really weird, really unrefined. Upvoted, but this needs a whole lot of trimming down and sentence structure changing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Now you mention it, I agree with you about the pictures. The first one is okay (though that might just be how much I like the caption), but the others are rather unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nothin like a fucked up dust zone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unrelated to my post in Page 1, i agree with the pictures sentiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is a whirlwind of what the fuck?

badumtsh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is a whirlwind of what the fuck?

e: Also, I think this is one of those rare times when the article would be better without the pictures and without the stuff trying to tie to the pictures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is a complete mess.

To summarize:

One day, several towns' worth of people turned into miserable dust-people, and everything there is dry and windy. Okay.

EXCEPT some people turned into animals instead, thus giving us an excuse to use two random unrelated pictures, but don't expect us to elaborate on the animal people even though they're the very first anomalous thing mentioned in the description. ALSO some random guy was lifted by birds while holding a birdcage what the hell, because we had yet another completely unrelated picture lying around we really wanted to use. ALSO the houses are teleporting and stuff. ALSO scissors attack. ALSO sounds of breaking glass, described in excessive detail. ALSO masked woman out of nowhere.

Oh golly, this is just trying to be way too many things at once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is creepy, well executed, hooked my neck and ripped off my nerves. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man. I'm glad I'm not the only one who thought the Dust Bowl was fucking creepy.

Appropriate music?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for imaginative imagery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually love this. But god fucking dammit Roget I wanted to use jumpdog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to like this, but it seems like it's too much weird shit going on everywhere and not enough reasons why. Don't get me wrong, I love it when crazy things go down in skips, and I actually liked all the different things individually; however, when all put together, it felt like a jumble of random anomalous things (especially with the very last addendum) that didn't do much for me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed a couple of ellipses to dashes, as it makes more sense for the doc in the interview to be getting interrupted rather than trailing off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sound have been compared to sheet glass impacting a wall

This should be either "sounds" or "has".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've done a pretty good job, but there are still a few places where your categorisation becomes a little unclear. Some of the references to SCP-1929 imply that it is a place rather than an event.

Apart from that, though, this looks good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't a huge fan of the interview, as it seemed to add stuff that didn't need to be added— in fact, you could probably slice off a paragraph or two of detail without losing anything valuable— but overall, this is one of those articles that I not only enjoyed, but wish I had written. +1

Reminds me a bit of the fantastic, Lynchian TV show Carnivale, which is set in the Dust Bowl era.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
stop

just

just stop with the writing already

tell us what you want and we'll give it to you, I swear

this kind of senseless writerbotism is devoid of meaning and purpose
